title: IllinoisBuddy
description: UIUC course planning web app aggregating Rate My Professor ratings, GPA distributions (Wade Fagen dataset), and Course Explorer API data. Flask + Google OAuth backend with React/TypeScript drag-and-drop multi-semester planner, GPA visualization, and schedule conflict detection. Migrating to PostgreSQL + FastAPI for production.

blog.subtitle: Building the course tool UIUC doesn't provide

sections.heading: What UIUC doesn't surface
paragraphs: Course Explorer is the official UIUC course system. It lists every class, every section, and every seat count, but it doesn't show GPA distributions, it doesn't tell you that section A has a different instructor than section B, and it doesn't tell you that the prereq you thought you had was changed last semester. Students already aggregate this in group chats and spreadsheets. IllinoisBuddy pulls it into one interface.

sections.heading: Three data sources
paragraphs: Course Explorer exposes an XML API with a deep but stable structure, and I wrote a client that crawls by subject, then course, then section, caching results in SQLite to avoid hammering the endpoint on every page load. Wade Fagen-Ulmschneider publishes a CSV of grade distributions per section from the university's records, joined on course ID and section letter, where the merge key isn't documented but trial and error on a sample resolved it. Rate My Professor has no official API, so the scraper runs server-side and review text is pulled live rather than stored, which is the only responsible way to handle someone else's review data.

sections.heading: What makes a planner real
paragraphs: A drag-and-drop multi-semester planner is straightforward on the frontend, but what makes it useful as a planning tool is the backend: prerequisites validated, credit hours checked against the term cap, time conflicts flagged, and the plan persisted across sessions so a student can draft their schedule in October and still see it in March. The first version used Flask with SQLite and Google OAuth to ship quickly. The in-progress second version uses FastAPI and PostgreSQL, because the first version's architecture is showing the kind of strain that signals a foundation needs to be replaced rather than extended.

sections.heading: Where this goes
paragraphs: The interesting version of this tool plans a full degree, not a single semester, which requires every graduation requirement from every catalog in a machine-readable format. UIUC doesn't publish that. Building the degree planner means scraping it, normalizing it across catalog years, and representing the prerequisite graph in a schema that can answer whether a student's planned sequence actually satisfies their major requirements. That work is in progress.
